mnemonics.title: P-P-S Pattern for Producer-Consumer
mnemonics.content: **P**roducers **P**roduce into shared buffer, **S**emaphores coordinate. Remember: wait(empty) before produce, signal(empty) after consume. This ensures proper empty/full tracking.

mnemonics.title: SEEN for Synchronization Elements
mnemonics.content: **S**emaphores for counting, **E**xclusion (mutex) for critical sections, **E**vents/Condition variables for blocking, **N**otification (signals) to wake waiters. SEEN covers all needed primitives.

mnemonics.title: CBC for Circular Buffer Code
mnemonics.content: When writing circular buffer code: **C**ount check → **B**lock or proceed → **C**ritical section. Check buffer state (full/empty), block if needed, then enter critical section to modify.

acronyms.expansion: First In First Out - the ordering property of producer-consumer buffers
acronyms.usage: Items produced first are consumed first, like a queue

acronyms.expansion: Last In First Out - alternative to FIFO using stack
acronyms.usage: Less common in producer-consumer, but used in stack-based work queues

acronyms.expansion: Compare And Swap - atomic instruction for lock-free algorithms
acronyms.usage: Used in lock-free producer-consumer to atomically update buffer head/tail pointers
Common in logging systems where many threads log to one writer
Simplest case, can use lock-free buffers efficiently

visualTips.visualization: Think of a clock face - head and tail are hour hand positions. When tail reaches 12 (buffer end), it wraps to 0. If head catches up to tail: empty (start=end). If tail is one position behind head in circular sense: full.
visualTips.purpose: Visualize wraparound behavior without modulo arithmetic confusion

visualTips.visualization: Two gauges: EMPTY meter (starts full at N, counts down as items added) and FULL meter (starts empty at 0, counts up as items added). Producers watch EMPTY gauge, consumers watch FULL gauge.
visualTips.purpose: Understand how semaphores track buffer state dynamically

visualTips.visualization: Factory assembly line: Producers are machines making widgets into a hopper (buffer). Consumers take widgets from hopper for packaging. Hopper size determines how far ahead producers can get before consumers must catch up.
visualTips.purpose: Understand bounded nature and flow control in real-world terms

analogies.analogy: A bus with fixed seats (N seats). Producers are passengers boarding, consumers are passengers alighting. Bus can't board if full, can't alight if empty. Seats = buffer slots.
Hotel checkout counter. EMPTY semaphore is like 'rooms available' count. When you checkout (consume), you increment rooms-available. When guest wants to check in (produce), they wait if no rooms.

Mutex is like a bathroom key - only one person can enter the critical section (use bathroom) at a time. Semaphore is like a count of available toilet paper rolls - tracks state, doesn't exclude entirely.

Restaurant kitchen. Orders (work items) go into a queue (buffer). Chefs (consumer threads) take orders to prepare. If queue full, orders wait at counter (producers blocked). If queue empty, chefs wait for orders.
